q: etcd in a Kubernetes cluster is:
opts: A load balancer for API requests
A distributed, consistent key-value store holding all cluster state
expl: etcd is the source of truth for the cluster: every object, every desired state, sits here. Losing etcd without a backup means losing the cluster state, which is why regular snapshots are critical.

q: Which component runs on every worker node and ensures containers described in PodSpecs are running?
expl: The kubelet is the node agent. It watches the API server for pods assigned to its node and instructs the container runtime (via CRI) to run them. kube-proxy handles network rules but does not manage containers.

q: A DaemonSet is best used when you need to:
opts: Run a batch job to completion
Run N replicas of a stateless web app
Run one pod per node (or subset of nodes) for cluster-wide agents
Run a one-shot task on a schedule
expl: DaemonSets guarantee a pod on every (or selected) node — ideal for log shippers (Fluent Bit), metrics agents (node-exporter), and CNI plugins. Deployments handle stateless replicas; Jobs run to completion; CronJobs run on schedule.

q: Which Service type exposes the service on each node's IP at a static port?
expl: NodePort opens a static port (30000-32767 by default) on every node that forwards to the Service. ClusterIP is internal-only, LoadBalancer provisions a cloud LB on top, ExternalName maps a DNS alias.

q: You need to provide a database password to a pod. Which object is most appropriate?
opts: A plain env var in the pod spec
expl: Secrets are designed for sensitive data — stored base64-encoded in etcd and can be encrypted at rest. ConfigMaps are for non-sensitive config. Plaintext env vars in the pod spec end up in version control and logs.

q: A pod is stuck in Pending. Which of these is the LEAST likely cause?
opts: Taints on nodes the pod does not tolerate
The container inside has crashed repeatedly
expl: A crashing container produces CrashLoopBackOff or Error — the pod has already been scheduled and started. Pending means it has not yet been scheduled, typically due to resources, taints, or unbound PVCs.

q: A Deployment directly manages which object?
expl: Deployment → ReplicaSet → Pods. During rolling updates, the Deployment creates a new ReplicaSet and scales down the old. You rarely manage ReplicaSets directly, but the hierarchy matters for the exam.

q: What is the defining purpose of a StatefulSet vs a Deployment?
opts: It provides stable network identities (predictable pod names) and stable persistent storage per replica
It only works on one node
expl: StatefulSets give pods ordinal, predictable names (web-0, web-1) and bind each to its own PVC. Essential for databases, brokers, and anything needing identity across restarts.

q: Which kubectl command applies configuration declaratively?
expl: apply is declarative — creates if absent, updates if present, tracks changes via annotations. create is imperative and errors when the resource already exists.

q: Labels and selectors are used to:
opts: Identify and group objects, enabling loose coupling between them
expl: Labels are key/value metadata; selectors query them. This is how a Service finds its pods, how a Deployment tracks its ReplicaSet, how NetworkPolicies target workloads. It is the glue of the Kubernetes object model.

q: A namespace in Kubernetes provides:
opts: A scope for resource names, enabling multi-tenant division of a cluster
expl: Namespaces scope resource names and quotas but are NOT a security boundary on their own. You need NetworkPolicies, RBAC, and possibly PodSecurity admission to actually isolate tenants.

q: Which statement about Pods is true?
opts: A pod must contain exactly one container
Containers in a pod share the network namespace and can share volumes
Each container in a pod has its own IP
expl: Pods can hold one OR more containers. They share an IP, localhost, and can mount the same volumes. Pods do not survive node failure — controllers (Deployments, StatefulSets) recreate them elsewhere.

q: A PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC) represents:
opts: A user's request for storage that binds to a PersistentVolume
A physical disk on a node
expl: PVCs decouple pods from the specifics of storage. A pod claims storage; the PVC binds to a matching PV (static or dynamically provisioned via a StorageClass). The pod never needs to know the storage backend.

q: An Ingress resource provides:
opts: HTTP/HTTPS routing rules from outside the cluster to internal Services
expl: Ingress defines rules (hosts, paths) routing external HTTP traffic to Services. It requires an Ingress controller (nginx, Traefik, HAProxy, etc.) to actually implement them — Ingress resources alone do nothing.

q: Which component maintains iptables/IPVS rules on nodes to implement the Service abstraction?
expl: kube-proxy watches Services and Endpoints on the API server and programs node network rules so traffic to a Service IP reaches a healthy pod. Modes: iptables, IPVS, nftables.

q: Taints and tolerations are used to:
opts: Repel pods from nodes unless the pod explicitly tolerates the taint
expl: A taint on a node says "keep pods away unless they tolerate me." Use cases: dedicated GPU nodes, reserved control plane nodes, nodes under maintenance. The inverse of node affinity (which attracts pods).

q: A ReplicaSet's core responsibility is to:
opts: Ensure a specified number of pod replicas are running at any time
expl: ReplicaSets ensure N replicas exist — nothing more. Rolling update logic lives in the Deployment above them. If you see "ReplicaSet" in a question, think "headcount enforcer."

q: ConfigMaps should NOT be used for:
expl: ConfigMaps store data in plaintext. For secrets, use Secrets, which (a) are handled with more care in logs and CLI output and (b) support at-rest encryption when configured.

q: When a pod is deleted, its IP address:
opts: Is reserved for that pod if recreated
Is released; a new pod gets a different IP
Is tied to the pod name
expl: Pod IPs are ephemeral. This is exactly why Services exist — they provide a stable virtual IP (ClusterIP) even as underlying pods come and go with different IPs.

q: Which is NOT a Kubernetes workload resource?
expl: LoadBalancer is a Service type, not a workload. Workload resources manage pods: Deployment, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob, ReplicaSet.

q: A CronJob creates:
expl: CronJob → schedule (cron syntax) → Job → Pod. Each scheduled firing spawns a Job that runs to completion.

q: How do control plane components typically talk to worker nodes?
opts: Through the kubelet on each node, which watches the API server
expl: All communication funnels through the API server. Kubelets on nodes watch the API server for assignments and report status back. Components do not talk to each other directly.

q: The Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) scales based on:
opts: CPU, memory, or custom/external metrics of pods
expl: HPA adjusts replica count based on observed pod metrics. CPU and memory are built-in; custom metrics come via adapters (metrics-server, Prometheus Adapter, KEDA for event-driven).

q: RBAC permissions are granted by which pair of resources?
expl: Roles define what can be done (namespaced) or ClusterRoles (cluster-scoped). Bindings attach those roles to subjects (users, groups, ServiceAccounts). You need both halves.

q: A container image tag `latest`:
opts: Is a mutable pointer that can change, making deployments non-reproducible
expl: `latest` is just a default tag — publishers may or may not update it. Production should pin to specific tags or, better, image digests (sha256:...) for true immutability.

q: A pod requests 250m CPU and 256Mi memory with no limits set. What does this mean?
opts: The pod is guaranteed those resources; can burst higher if node capacity allows
It will be OOM-killed if it exceeds 256Mi
expl: Requests = scheduler reservation (guaranteed floor). Limits = cap. Without limits, a pod can use whatever the node has free — which risks noisy-neighbor effects and unpredictable OOM behavior. Best practice: set both.

q: Which probe determines if a container is ready to receive traffic from a Service?
expl: Readiness failure removes the pod from Service endpoints (but does not restart). Liveness failure restarts the container. Startup probe disables the other two until the app finishes starting.

q: Which interface standardizes how kubelet talks to container runtimes?
expl: CRI = Container Runtime Interface, gRPC API between kubelet and runtimes (containerd, CRI-O). CSI is storage, CNI is networking, OCI specifies image and runtime formats.

q: CNI plugins are responsible for:
opts: Assigning IPs and configuring pod network connectivity
expl: CNI plugins (Calico, Cilium, Flannel, Weave) set up pod network interfaces, assign IPs, and program routes. Every cluster picks one primary CNI.

q: CSI exists so that:
opts: Containers can share storage without volumes
Storage vendors can plug into Kubernetes (and other orchestrators) without modifying core code
Containers are isolated from each other
expl: CSI decouples storage drivers from the Kubernetes release cycle. EBS, Ceph, Portworx, Longhorn all ship their own CSI drivers that K8s loads at runtime — no core patches needed.

q: OCI (Open Container Initiative) defines:
opts: Container image format (image-spec) and runtime behavior (runtime-spec)
expl: OCI is a Linux Foundation project. Its two main specs — image-spec and runtime-spec — are why an image built with Docker also runs under containerd, CRI-O, or Podman. Plus distribution-spec for registries.

q: A NetworkPolicy in Kubernetes:
opts: Is enforced automatically by the API server
Is enforced by the CNI plugin — and is silently ignored if the CNI does not support it
expl: This is a gotcha. NetworkPolicy is a K8s resource, but enforcement is delegated to the CNI. Flannel does not enforce by default; Calico, Cilium, and Weave do. Apply a policy without an enforcing CNI and traffic flows freely.

q: Which Pod Security Standard is most restrictive?
expl: Three levels: Privileged (unrestricted), Baseline (prevents known privilege escalation), Restricted (follows hardening best practices — non-root, no host mounts, etc.). "Enforced" is a mode, not a level.

q: Which tool scales the number of nodes in a cluster based on pending pods?
expl: Cluster Autoscaler watches for pods that cannot be scheduled due to resource shortage and requests more nodes from the cloud provider. HPA = pod replicas; VPA = pod resource requests.

q: CoreDNS in a Kubernetes cluster provides:
opts: DNS-based service discovery for pods and services
expl: CoreDNS is the default K8s DNS server. It answers records like my-svc.my-ns.svc.cluster.local so pods can reach Services by name without knowing their ClusterIPs.

q: Which volume type is erased when the pod is removed from a node?
expl: emptyDir lives and dies with the pod. Useful for scratch space, intermediate processing, or sharing between containers in the same pod. For durability, use PVCs.

q: An admission controller runs:
opts: After authN/authZ, before the request is persisted to etcd
expl: Admission controllers are the last gatekeepers before persistence. They can validate (reject invalid requests) or mutate (inject sidecars, default values). Webhooks let you plug in custom policy.

q: Which is NOT typically a container runtime responsibility?
expl: Scheduling is kube-scheduler territory (control plane). Runtimes operate at the node level: image mgmt, lifecycle, isolation (via cgroups, namespaces), in cooperation with CNI and CSI plugins.

q: Core principles of the Twelve-Factor App include:
opts: A shared database across all services
Config in env variables, backing services as attached resources, stateless processes
expl: Twelve-Factor is a SaaS methodology. Key factors for cloud native: III. Config in env, IV. Backing services as resources, VI. Stateless processes, XI. Logs as event streams. It is why containers fit so well.

q: Serverless (FaaS) means:
opts: Developers don't manage infra; the platform provisions compute on demand and can scale to zero
Code runs only on edge devices
expl: Servers still exist — they are just the provider's problem. Pay for execution, not idle capacity. Scale-to-zero is a defining property. Examples: AWS Lambda, Knative, OpenFaaS.

q: Which CNCF project is a Kubernetes-native serverless platform?
expl: Knative adds serverless primitives to Kubernetes: autoscaling (including to zero), event sources, and simplified deployment. OpenFaaS is another option in the ecosystem.

q: A service mesh primarily provides:
opts: Service-to-service mTLS, observability, and traffic management via sidecar proxies
expl: Meshes (Istio, Linkerd, Consul, Kuma) inject a sidecar proxy (usually Envoy) into each pod. The proxy handles mTLS, retries, circuit breaking, traffic splitting, and emits telemetry — all without app code changes.

q: Site Reliability Engineering (SRE) treats operations as:
opts: A software engineering problem, using error budgets and SLOs to balance reliability and feature velocity
expl: SRE was born at Google. Key idea: error budget = 100% − SLO. When you burn your budget, you slow feature work until reliability recovers. Treats ops problems with software solutions.

q: A key trade-off of microservices architecture is:
opts: Independent deployability, but higher operational complexity (distributed systems challenges)
expl: Microservices give teams autonomy but introduce distributed systems pain: network failures, eventual consistency, harder debugging. Do not pick microservices for small teams or simple domains — monoliths are often the right answer.

q: FinOps in a cloud native context refers to:
opts: A practice for managing and attributing cloud spend
expl: FinOps brings financial accountability to the variable cost model of cloud. In K8s, tools like OpenCost (CNCF) and Kubecost allocate spend back to namespaces, teams, or workloads.
